topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. *Effective immediately, the university will cease all operations.* I reread the subject line three times, the letters swimming before my eyes. My hands started shaking so violently I had to grip the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white. A strangled sound escaped my throat, somewhere between a gasp and a sob. I stood up, pacing the small dorm room like a caged animal, kicking over a stack of textbooks. The fluorescent lights above seemed to buzz with an unbearable, high-pitched whine. "No. No, this isn't happening," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. I started running through the campus map in my head, tracing routes that suddenly felt like traps, my breath coming in ragged, shallow bursts. Every shadow seemed to twist into a looming figure.

[story 2]

I stared at the official notice tacked to the community board, the ink blurring into meaningless shapes. My vision tunneled. The air in the lecture hall felt thick, suffocating, and I couldn't draw a full breath. A wave of nausea washed over me, so intense it made my knees buckle. I dropped the paper, and it fluttered to the linoleum floor. I began to laugh, a sharp, uncontrolled sound that quickly devolved into choked, hiccuping sobs. I clawed at my hair, pulling at strands until my scalp throbbed. "They can't just—they can't just erase everything!" I shouted into the empty corridor, my voice cracking with raw disbelief, my chest heaving with frantic, uneven rhythm.

[story 3]

The phone call had been the final straw. The voice on the other end, calm and utterly devoid of empathy, had delivered the news in a monotone that somehow amplified the disaster. I clutched the receiver so tightly my fingers went numb. My entire body felt electrified, vibrating with a frantic energy that had nowhere to go. I began to pace the length of my kitchen, my steps quick and jerky, my gaze darting wildly toward the window, as if expecting the building itself to dissolve. Tears welled up instantly, hot and stinging, blurring the view of the familiar neighborhood. "This is a joke," I muttered, shaking my head rapidly, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. I dropped the phone onto the counter, the resulting clatter sounding deafening in the sudden, terrifying silence.
